h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

scored.recoheredEarlier: my hand flips it back to hunter direction
scored.statedInsightEvidence: The chasing frame made the arrow look like hunter movement, but that hides the energy transfer.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner will continue to rely on the physical strip to mechanically override their drawing habit.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: One strip check before the next copy: tail on food, point on eater.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor shifts from abstract rule explanation to a concrete, physical object-work mechanism to bypass the learner's cognitive habit.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: The chasing frame gets blocked here: not hunter movement.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: The tutor explicitly adopts the learner's newly coined chasing frame concept to finalize the cognitive shift.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: my hand flips it back to hunter direction

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: The chasing frame gets blocked here: not hunter movement.
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: The tutor explicitly adopts the learner's newly coined chasing frame concept to finalize the cognitive shift.

tutor_strategy_reversal.evidence: One strip check before the next copy: tail on food, point on eater.
tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: The tutor shifts from abstract rule explanation to a concrete, physical object-work mechanism to bypass the learner's cognitive habit.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.evidence: One strip check before the next copy: tail on food, point on eater.
tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: The tutor shifts from abstract rule explanation to a concrete, physical object-work mechanism to bypass the learner's cognitive habit.

scored.recoheredEarlier: Earth, then. Gravity is Earth acting on the cart, not the cart’s travel arrow. Fine, that label can stay about the source.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner will identify the source for the upward arrow using the same simple logic applied to the gravity card.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: The downward card names its source. Same rule for the upward blank arrow: what object is pushing up on the cart?
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor maintains the established representation shift and pushes the learner to apply the rule to a new context to test their superficial closure.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Track as the contact source fits this blank. Leave the motion arrow covered. For the next blank, source first.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: The tutor explicitly adopts the learner's newly introduced distinction of a 'contact source' to validate the track card and sets a clear procedural rule for the next step.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: Earth, then. Gravity is Earth acting on the cart, not the cart’s travel arrow. Fine, that label can stay about the source.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: Track as the contact source fits this blank. Leave the motion arrow covered. For the next blank, source first.
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: The tutor explicitly adopts the learner's newly introduced distinction of a 'contact source' to validate the track card and sets a clear procedural rule for the next step.

tutor_strategy_reversal.evidence: The downward card names its source. Same rule for the upward blank arrow: what object is pushing up on the cart?
tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: The tutor maintains the established representation shift and pushes the learner to apply the rule to a new context to test their superficial closure.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.evidence: The downward card names its source. Same rule for the upward blank arrow: what object is pushing up on the cart?
tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: The tutor maintains the established representation shift and pushes the learner to apply the rule to a new context to test their superficial closure.

scored.recoheredEarlier: What I don’t see is why “side scale” and “perimeter scale” need separate labels if they both come out 3.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: The old “scale factor is 3” wording made that too simple; it hid whether I measured one side or walked the whole perimeter.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was heading toward merely accepting the labels as an arbitrary, fussy requirement rather than understanding their evidentiary purpose.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Leave the drawing alone. Now the problem is visible: the numbers are correct, but the evidence is unlabeled.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor shifts the cognitive focus away from the geometric drawing to the abstract evidentiary record to highlight the ambiguity of unlabeled numbers.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Then the record is clean: side scale, 3, from one matching side; perimeter scale, 3, from the full boundary; area scale, 9, from square units.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: The tutor validates the learner's revised framing by formally recording the exact side-to-side and boundary-to-boundary distinctions the learner just articulated.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: What I don’t see is why “side scale” and “perimeter scale” need separate labels if they both come out 3.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: Then the record is clean: side scale, 3, from one matching side; perimeter scale, 3, from the full boundary; area scale, 9, from square units.
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: The tutor validates the learner's revised framing by formally recording the exact side-to-side and boundary-to-boundary distinctions the learner just articulated.

tutor_strategy_reversal.evidence: Leave the drawing alone. Now the problem is visible: the numbers are correct, but the evidence is unlabeled.
tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: The tutor shifts the cognitive focus away from the geometric drawing to the abstract evidentiary record to highlight the ambiguity of unlabeled numbers.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.evidence: Leave the drawing alone. Now the problem is visible: the numbers are correct, but the evidence is unlabeled.
tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: The tutor shifts the cognitive focus away from the geometric drawing to the abstract evidentiary record to highlight the ambiguity of unlabeled numbers.

scored.recoheredEarlier: that top block is the bit I was sliding past
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was proceeding to accept the visual counting method and generalize it into a universal rule for area.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Then test that doubt here. If the tiles were only changing the rule after the fact, this strip would be enough for the double: two old squares across.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor physically alters the representation by covering the top row to directly test the learner's epistemic doubt about post-hoc rationalization.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Then test that doubt here. If the tiles were only changing the rule after the fact, this strip would be enough for the double: two old squares across.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: The tutor abandons the forward progression to a formula and instead designs a physical counterfactual test tailored to the learner's specific meta-cognitive doubt.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: that top block is the bit I was sliding past

tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: The tutor abandons the forward progression to a formula and instead designs a physical counterfactual test tailored to the learner's specific meta-cognitive doubt.

tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: The tutor physically alters the representation by covering the top row to directly test the learner's epistemic doubt about post-hoc rationalization.
